text: <sup>[This letter is from]</sup> Paul, a prisoner of Christ Jesus, and Timothy our brother, <sup>[and is being sent]</sup> to Philemon our dearly loved <sup>[brother]</sup> and fellow-worker,

and to Apphia our sister <sup>[Note: This was probably Philemon’s wife]</sup>, and to Archippus our fellow-soldier <sup>[Note: This was probably Philemon’s preacher son. See Col. 4:17]</sup> and to the church <sup>[that meets]</sup> in your house.
May there be unearned favor to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
I always thank my God <sup>[for you]</sup> as I mention you in my prayers,
hearing of your love for all saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup>, and your faith toward the Lord Jesus.
<sup>[I pray]</sup> that the sharing of your faith <sup>[with others, or possibly with Paul]</sup> will be effective when you fully realize all the good you can do for Christ.
For I have much joy and encouragement from <sup>[observing]</sup> your love, because you have cheered the hearts of the saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup>, brother.
Therefore, although in <sup>[the fellowship of]</sup> Christ I have enough boldness to order you to do the proper thing,

yet out of <sup>[mutual]</sup> love, I appeal to you <sup>[to do what I ask]</sup>. For I Paul, am an old man, and now also a prisoner of Christ Jesus. <sup>[Note: Most calculations suggest that Paul was approximately 60 years old, which was considered a relatively advanced age in that day]</sup>.
I appeal to you on behalf of my son, Onesimus, whom I <sup>[spiritually]</sup> conceived while <sup>[here]</sup> in prison.
He was once useless to me, but is now useful to <sup>[both]</sup> you and me. <sup>[Note: Since the name Onesimus means “useful,” it appears Paul is making an intentional play on the word]</sup>.
<sup>[Now]</sup> I am sending him back to you in person, <sup>[as though he were]</sup> part of my very self.
I really wanted to keep him <sup>[here]</sup> with me so that, on your behalf, he could minister to me while in prison for the sake of the Gospel.
But I did not want to do anything without your permission, so that your goodness <sup>[of heart]</sup> would not be shown out of a feeling of obligation, but out of willingness.
For possibly Onesimus left you for a short time so that you could have him <sup>[back]</sup> forever,

not as a slave <sup>[only]</sup>, but more than a slave, as a dearly loved brother. <sup>[He is that]</sup> to me especially, but how much more to you, both in a physical way <sup>[i.e., by continuing to serve as your slave]</sup> and in <sup>[the fellowship of]</sup> the Lord <sup>[i.e., now also as your brother in Christ]</sup>.
If then you consider me to be a partner <sup>[in the faith]</sup>, welcome him <sup>[back]</sup> as <sup>[you would]</sup> me.
But if he has done anything wrong to you, or owes you any <sup>[money]</sup>, charge it to my account.
I, Paul, am writing this in my own handwriting: I will pay you back. <sup>[But]</sup> I will not mention that you owe me your own self, as well.
So, brother, I would like to have your help in <sup>[the fellowship of]</sup> the Lord; cheer me up in Christ <sup>[i.e., by welcoming Onesimus back on good terms]</sup>.
I am writing you, confident that you will be obedient <sup>[to my requests]</sup>, and I know that you will do even more than I am asking <sup>[of you]</sup>.

And another thing: Prepare a place for me to stay, because I hope that through <sup>[an answer to]</sup> your prayers I will be returned to you. <sup>[Note: Paul was hoping to be released from imprisonment to visit Philemon at Colosse]</sup>.
Epaphras, my fellow-prisoner in <sup>[the service of]</sup> Christ sends you his greetings,

along with <sup>[John]</sup> Mark <sup>[Note: This shows that Mark was now back in the good graces of Paul. See Acts 15:36-40]</sup>, Aristarchus, Demas <sup>[Note: This man later deserted Paul. See II Tim. 4:10]</sup> and Luke, who are <sup>[also]</sup> my fellow-workers. <sup>[Note: These men, unlike Epaphras who was imprisoned with Paul, were free to minister for Christ there in Rome]</sup>.
May the unearned favor of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. May it be so.
